cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The genetic transfer of characteristics from parents to offspring.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: every nongenetic influence, from prenatal nutrition to the people and things around us

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: An approach to psychological inquiry that views human cognition and behavior in a Darwinian context of adaptation to physical and social environments and new intellectual challenges.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: a philosophy that seeks to eradicate genetic defects and improve the genetic makeup of populations through selective human breeding.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The brain and spinal cord.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The sensory and motor neurons that connect the CNS to the rest of the body.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The part of the peripheral nervous system that controls the glands and muscles of the internal organs.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The division of the PNS that controls the body's skeletal muscles. Also called the skeletal nervous system.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The division of the ANS that calms the body, conserving its energy.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The division of the autonomic nervous system that arouses the body, mobilizing its energy.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A nerve cell; the basic building block of the nervous system.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Cells in the nervous system that support, nourish, and protect neurons. They play a role in learning, thinking, and memory.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: the nerve pathway involved in a reflex action \- at its simplest, a sensory nerve and a motor nerve with a synapse between.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Also known as afferent neurons. Neurons that carry incoming information from the body's tissues and sensory receptors to the brain and spinal cord.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Also known as efferent neurons. Neurons that carry outgoing information from the brain and spinal cord to the muscles and glands.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Neurons witin the brain and spinal cord; they communicate internally and process information between the sensory inputs and motor outputs.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A neuron's reaction of either firing (with a full-strength response) or not firing.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: a reduction in the electric potential across the plasma membrane of a neuron, such that the inner surface of the membrane becomes less negative in relation to the outer surface.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: In neural processing, a brief resting pause that occurs after a neuron has fired; subsequent action potentials cannot occur until the axon returns to its resting state.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A neurotransmitter's reabsorption by the sending neuron.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: a chronic, progressive disease involving damage to the sheaths of nerve cells in the brain and spinal cord. Symptoms may include numbness, impairment of speech and of muscular coordination, blurred vision, and severe fatigue.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The level of stimulation required to trigger a neural impulse.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: an autoimmune disorder in which the body produces antibodies against acetylcholine receptors, causing faulty transmission of nerve impulses at neuromuscular junctions.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Chemical messengers that cross the synaptic gaps between neurons. When released, they bind to receptor sites on the receiving neuron, thereby influencing whether that neuron will generate a neural impulse.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Chemicals that depolarize postsynaptic neurons, resulting in a greater likelihood of an action potential.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: chemicals that hyperpolarize the postsynaptic neurons, resulting in a lessened likelihood of an action potential.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: a neurotransmitter that has an important role in motor behavior and is implicated in numerous mental conditions and emotional states, as well as within the brain's reward center. Abnormalities regarding this neurotransmitter are associated with Parkinson's Disease, ADHD, and Schizophrenia.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A common neurotransmitter with roles in emotional processing, mood, appetite, and sleep. It is implicated in many psychological conditions, including depressive disorders, anxiety disorders, sleep disorders, aggression, and psychosis.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A chemical classified as both a hormone and neurotransmitter that assists in the regulation of arousal, attention, cognitive function, and stress reactions.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The predominant excitatory neurotransmitter in the brain. Plays a critical role in cognitive, motor, and sensory functions.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: a major inhibitory neurotransmitter in the nervous system.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Neurotransmitters produced in the brain that block the perception of pain and increase feelings of wellbeing.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A neurotransmitter that is involved in the transmission of pain messages to the brain.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: a major neurotransmitter that plays an important role in memory formation and learning. Is implicated in both Alzheimer's disease and myasthenia gravis.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A chemical messenger that is manufactured by endocrine glands, travel through the bloodstream, and affect other tissues.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A neurotransmitter and hormone that is secreted in large amounts when an individual is stimulated by fear, anxiety, or a similar stress-related situation. Increases the heart rate and force of heart contractions, relaxes bronchial and intestinal smooth muscle, and produces varying effects on blood pressure.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A hormone produced by fat cells acting mainly in the regulation of appetite and fat storage.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A hunger-arousing hormone secreted by an empty stomach.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A hormone manufactured by the pineal gland that produces sleepiness.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: a hormone released by the pituitary gland that causes increased contraction of the uterus during labor and stimulates the ejection of milk into the ducts of the breasts. Important for the formation of attachment between mother and child.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Any substance that has significant effects on psychological processes, such as thinking, perception, and emotion.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A molecule that increases a neurotransmitter's action.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A molecule that inhibits or blocks a neurotransmitter's action.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Drugs that excite neural activity and speed up body functions.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Drugs that reduce neural activity and slow body functions.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Psychedelic, "mind manifesting" drugs that distort perceptions and evoke sensory images in the absence of sensory input.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Opium and its derivatives; drugs that depress neural activity, temporarily lessening pain and anxiety.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The diminishing effect with regular use of the same dose of a drug, requiring the user to take larger and larger doses before experiencing the drug's effect.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The everyday term for compulsive substance use.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The central core of the brain, beginning where the spinal cord swells as it enters the skull; responsible for automatic survival functions.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The hindbrain structure that is the brainstem's base; controls heart rate and breathing.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The nerve network that travels through the brain stem and into the thalamus; it filters information and plays an important role in controlling arousal.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The hindbrain's "little brain" at the rear of the brainstem; its functions include processing sensory input, coordinating movement output and balance, and enabling nonverbal learning and memory.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The intricate fabric of interconnected neural cells covering the forebrain's cerebral hemispheres; the body's ultimate control and information-processing center.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Neural system located mostly in the forebrain \- below the cerebral hemispheres \- that includes the amygdala, hypothalamus, hippocampus, thalamus, and pituitary gland; associated with emotions and drives.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The forebrain's sensory control center, located on top of the brainstem; it directs messages to the sensory receiving areas in the cortex and transmits replies to the cerebellum and medulla.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A limbic system neural structure lying below the thalamus. It directs several maintenance activities (eating, drinking, body temperature), helps govern the endocrine system, and is linked to emotion and reward.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Two lima bean sized neural clusters in the limbic system, linked with emotion.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The endocrine system's most influential gland. Under the influence of the hypothalamus, the it regulates growth and controls other endocrine glands.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A neural center located in the limbic system; helps process explicit memories \- of facts and events \- for storage.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The large band of neural fibers connecting the two brain hemispheres and carrying messages between them.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The portion of the cerebral cortex lying at the back of the head; it includes areas that receive information from the visual fields.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The portion of the cerebral cortex lying at the top of the head and toward the rear; it receives sensory input for touch and body position.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: portion of the cerebral cortex lying just behind the forehead. Enables linguistic processing, muscle movements, higher-order thinking, and executive functioning (such as making plans and decisions)

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The portion of the cerebral cortex lying roughly above the ears. It includes the auditory areas, which receive information primarily from the opposite ear. The left hemisphere also enables language processing.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A cerebral cortex area at the rear of the frontal lobe athat controls voluntary movement.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A frontal lobe brain area, usually in the left hemisphere, that helps control language expression by directing the muscle movements involved in speech.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A brain area, usually in the left temporal lobe, involved in language comprehension and expression.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Surgical cutting of the corpus callosum to study the effects of disconnecting the right and left brain hemispheres \- specifically, the independent functioning of the two hemispheres.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The brain's ability to change, especially during childhood, by reorganizing after damage or by building new pathways.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The organization of the brain into right and left hemispheres, with each hemisphere performing unique and specialized functions.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: An amplified recording of the waves of electrical activity sweeping across the brain's surface. These waves are measured by electrodes placed on the scalp.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A technique for revealing blood flow, and therefore, brain activity by comparing successive MRI scans. Show function as well as structure.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Tissue destruction. May occur naturally, during surgery, or experimentally.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: a technique that uses magnetic fields and radio waves to produce computer-generated images of soft tissue. Show brain anatomy.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: the biological clock; regular bodily rhythms (for example, of temperature and wakefulness) that occur on a 24-hour cycle

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Quiet, typically dreamless sleep in which rapid eye movements are absent; divided into four stages; also called quiet sleep.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A recurring sleep stage during which vivid dreams commonly occur. Also known as paradoxical sleep, because the muscles are relaxed (except for minor twitches) but other body systems are active.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Theory that dreams reflect inputs from brain activation originating in the pons, which the forebrain then attempts to weave into a story

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that circuits wired together during the waking period are consolidated, or strengthened, during sleep

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Tecurring problems in falling or staying asleep

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A sleep disorder characterized by uncontrollable sleep attacks. The sufferer may lapse directly into REM sleep, often at inopportune times.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A neurological disorder in which the person does not become paralyzed during REM sleep and thus acts out dreams.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: a sleep disorder characterized by temporary cessations of breathing during sleep and repeated momentary awakenings

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The condition of walking or performing some other activity without awakening; also known as sleepwalking

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The process by which our sensory receptors and nervous system receive and represent stimulus energies from our environment

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The minimum intensity of stimulation that must occur before you experience a sensation.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: the threshold at which one can distinguish two stimuli that are of different intensities.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A decrease in sensitivity to a constant level of stimulation

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: the principle that, to be perceived as different, two stimuli must differ by a constant minimum percentage (rather than a constant amount).

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A condition in which stimulation of one sense generates a simultaneous sensation in another.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The light-sensitive inner surface of the eye, containing the receptor rods and cones plus layers of neurons that begin the processing of visual information.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The point at which the optic nerve leaves the eye, creating a "blind" spot because there are no receptor cells located there.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: In sensation, the process by which the eye's lens changes shape to focus images of near or far objects on the retina.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that the retina contains three different types of color receptors \- one most sensitive to red, one to green, and one to blue \- which, when stimulated in combination, can produce the perception of any color.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that opposing retinal processes (red-green, blue-yellow, black-white) enable color vision. For example, some cells are stimulated by green and inhibited by red, but others may be stimulated by red and inhibited by green.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The central focal point in the retina, around which the eye's cones cluster.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Retinal receptors that are concentrated near the center of the retina and that function in daylight or well-lit conditions. Cones detect detail and give rise to color sensations.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A visual image that persists after a stimulus is removed.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Retinal receptors that detect black, white, and gray, and are sensitive to movement; necessary for peripheral and twilight vision, when cones don't respond.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: the specialized cells whose axons form the optic nerve, which takes the information to the brain

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A type of color deficiency in which one cone type is missing or malfunctioning.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A type of color deficiency in which two cone types are missing or malfunctioning.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Also known as face blindness \- an inability to recognize faces.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A condition in which a person can respond to a visual stimulus without consciously experiencing it.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The height of a light or sound wave. Determines brightness in light and loudness in sound.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The distance from the peak of one light or sound wave to the peak of the next. Determines hue in light, and pitch in sound.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: In hearing, the theory that links the pitch we hear with the place where the cochlea's membrane is stimulated. Also known as place coding.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that groups of neurons in the auditory system respond to a sound by firing action potentials slightly out of phase with one another so that when combined, a greater frequency of sound can be encoded and sent to the brain to be analyzed.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: In hearing, the theory that the rate of nerve impulses traveling up the auditory nerve matches the frequency of the tone, thus enabling us to sense its pitch. Also called temporal coding.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: the ability to identify the position and changes in position of sound sources based on acoustic information \- relies on the use of both ears.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A less common form of hearing loss, caused by damage to the mechanical system that conducts sound waves to the cochlea.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The most common form of hearing loss, caused by damage to the cochlea's receptor cells or to the auditory nerve. Also called nerve deafness.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Our sensation of smell

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Our sensation of taste

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: the brain center that processes smell, located below the frontal lobes

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: rough, bumpy elevations on surface of tongue; sensory receptors that send signals for taste to the brain.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The theory that the spinal cord contains a neurological "gate" that blocks pain signals or allows them to pass on to the brain. The "gate" is opened by the activity of pain signals traveling up small nerve fibers and is closed by activity in larger fibers or information coming from the brain.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: the perception of sensations or pain in an amputated limb.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Our balance sense; our sense of body movement and position that enables our equilibrium

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: out movement sense \- our system for sensing the position and movement of individual body parts.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A process in which individuals that have certain inherited traits tend to survive and reproduce at higher rates than other individuals because of those traits.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A research design in which hereditary influence is assessed by comparing the resemblance of identical twins and fraternal twins with respect to a trait.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The state of the neuron when not firing a neural impulse.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A stimulant found in coffee, tea, cola drinks, chocolate, and many over-the-counter medications

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A common depressant.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The discomfort, distress, and physical symptoms that follow discontinuing the use of an addictive drug.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A drug, often smoked, whose effects include euphoria, impairment of judgment and concentration and occasionally hallucinations.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: A dopamine-rich pathway in the brain that produces feelings of pleasure when activated.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The half of the cerebrum concerned with spatial cognition, facial recognition, visual processing, and emotional processes, among other functions. Controls the opposite side of the body.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: an area at the front of the parietal lobes that registers and processes body touch and movement sensations

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: impairment of language, usually caused by left hemisphere damage either to Broca's or Wernicke's area.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: a powerful and addictive stimulant, derived from the coca plant, producing temporarily increased alertness and euphoria

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The half of the cerebrum concerned with speech, language, and analytical functions, among other processes. Controls the opposite side of the body.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Part of brain stem involved in arousal and attention, sleep and wakefulness, and control of reflexes.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The intricate fabric of interconnected neural cells covering the cerebral hemispheres; the body's ultimate control and information-processing center.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: the "master gland" of the body's endocrine system, which releases hormones that direct the functions of many other glands in the body.

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The large band of neural fibers connecting the two brain hemispheres and carrying messages between them

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: An area at the rear of the frontal lobes that controls voluntary movement

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: Controls language reception \- a brain area involved in language comprehension and expression; usually in the left temporal lobe

cards.subject: ap-psychology
cards.prompt: The brain's ability to change, especially during childhood, by reorganizing after damage or by building new pathways based on experience
